title: Writing - Part 1: Gender Equality in the Workplace
description: Your class has listened to a talk on gender equality in the workplace and the obstacles that continue to prevent equal opportunities for men and women in professional environments.

content.question: Write an essay discussing two of the issues in your notes. You should explain which issue you consider most urgent to address, giving reasons for your opinion.

opinions: Women still earn less than men for doing the same work.
Women are still dramatically underrepresented in senior leadership roles.
Caregiving responsibilities at home continue to hold women's careers back.

structure.introduction: Introduce the topic of gender equality at work and identify the two issues you will discuss.
structure.bodyParagraph1: Analyse the first issue (e.g. the gender pay gap), discussing its causes and consequences.
structure.bodyParagraph2: Examine the second issue (e.g. underrepresentation in leadership), explaining why you consider it the most urgent priority.
structure.conclusion: Summarise your analysis and make a clear statement about which issue requires most immediate action and why.

content.modelAnswer:
Despite considerable progress over recent decades, genuine equality between men and women in the workplace remains an aspiration rather than a reality in most parts of the world. Two issues that lie at the heart of this problem are the persistent gender pay gap and the chronic underrepresentation of women in leadership positions.

The gender pay gap reflects a complex web of causes, including occupational segregation, discrimination and the disproportionate career disruption caused by childcare responsibilities. In many countries, legislation requiring pay transparency has shed light on the scale of the disparity, yet change has been frustratingly slow. Beyond the economic injustice to individual women, the gap sends a damaging signal that female labour is inherently less valuable, perpetuating a cultural norm that is difficult to dismantle.

In my view, however, the underrepresentation of women in senior leadership demands the most urgent attention. When women are absent from boardrooms and executive teams, the decisions shaping organisations — including those relating to pay, promotion and flexible working — are made without adequate input from half the workforce. Research consistently demonstrates that gender-diverse leadership improves organisational performance, yet women account for fewer than a quarter of board members in most major economies. Without deliberate intervention, such as transparent promotion criteria and mentoring initiatives, progress will remain negligible.

In conclusion, addressing both the pay gap and leadership representation is essential, but transforming who holds power within organisations is the more fundamental step, as it creates the conditions for all other inequalities to be meaningfully challenged.
